---
title: "Activity: Ensembles of Decision Trees"
subtitle: "Math 437"
format: html
editor: source
---

## Data

For regression, we'll continue to use our Lahman baseball salary data. For classification, we'll use the RateMyProfessor data.

```{r}
#| label: load packages
library(Lahman)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(randomForest) # for book labs
library(ranger) # for these examples
library(gbm) # for book labs
library(xgboost) # what people actually use
library(vip) # variable importance plot
```

```{r}
#| label: load batting data
salaries <- Salaries %>%
  dplyr::select(playerID, yearID, teamID, salary)
peopleInfo <- People %>%
  dplyr::select(playerID, birthYear, birthMonth, nameLast,
         nameFirst, bats)
batting <- battingStats() %>% 
  left_join(salaries, 
            by =c("playerID", "yearID", "teamID")) %>%
  left_join(peopleInfo, by = "playerID") %>%
  mutate(age = yearID - birthYear - 
           1L *(birthMonth >= 10)) %>%
  arrange(playerID, yearID, stint)

batting_2016 <- batting %>% filter(yearID == 2016,
                                   !is.na(salary),
                                   G >= 100, AB >= 200
                                   ) %>%
  mutate(salary = log10(salary), # there's a reason for this, I promise
         lgID = factor(lgID, levels = c("AL", "NL"))) # fix the defunct league issue

set.seed(11249)
batting_2016_split <- initial_split(batting_2016, prop = 0.75)
batting_train <- training(batting_2016_split)
batting_test <- testing(batting_2016_split)
# set up kfold CV here
batting_kfold <- vfold_cv(batting_train, v = 5, repeats = 3) 
```

```{r}
#| label: load RMP data
RMP <- readr::read_csv("../Data Sets/RateMyProfessor.csv")
RMP$hotness <- factor(RMP$hotness, levels = c("hot", "cold"))
RMP$rank <- factor(RMP$rank, 
                   levels = c("Assistant Professor", "Associate Professor", "Professor"),
                   labels = c("Asst", "Assoc", "Full"))
set.seed(1880)
RMP_split <- initial_split(RMP, prop = 0.80) 

RMP_train <- training(RMP_split)
RMP_test <- testing(RMP_split)
# set up kfold CV here
RMP_kfold <- vfold_cv(RMP_train, v = 5, repeats = 3)
```


## Bagging and Random Forests(TM)

Decision trees are great for interpretability but not great for prediction, mostly because they are incredibly high-variance. However, recall that in general we can reduce variance by averaging. This means that if we grow a whole bunch of terrible high-variance trees in parallel and average their predictions, we can reduce variance while not affecting bias much.

We generally have two options for growing trees in parallel: bagging and random forests. The default tidymodels engine for both methods is  the `ranger` package. However, there is also an option to use the `randomForest` package, which is what the book uses. There are a few minor differences in terms of what comes out of the model, but generally, just pick one and `set_engine` to that one.

### Understanding the Algorithm

1. If we fit 500 decision trees on the same training set, we will get 500 of the same exact tree. How do we ensure the trees are different?
Bagging comes from Bootstrap Aggregation
1. First we take a bootstrap sample
2. Fit a decision tree.
3. Repeat steps 1-2 B(500ish) times.
4. For each obs. in the test set predict on all the trees and avg. the predictions.


Random Forests
1. Take a bootstrap sample.
2. Fit a decision tree, but each time we choose how to split we restrict the algorithm to only m < p predictors as possibilities 
typically m = rad p or m = m/3 as defaults
3-4 as in bagging
this should decorrelate the predictions



There are three parameters that we can tune:

* `trees` indicates the number of trees to fit - the default is 500
* `mtry` indicates the number of predictors that will be randomly selected at each split - the default varies between engines
* `min_n` determines whether a node is a terminal node (if there are fewer `min_n` observations at a node, then the node is a terminal node) - the default is 5 for regression and 10 for classification

The main difference between random forests and bagging is in `mtry`: for bagging, we explicitly set `mtry` to the number of predictors in the dataset, while for random forests, we tune the value of `mtry`. 

2. Why might tuning the value of `mtry` and not using every predictor on every split result in better predictions?

### Bagging for Regression

The model for bagging and random forests is `rand_forest`. Because we are doing some bootstrapping, we need to set a seed for reproducibility. We will set this using the `set_args` function so that it carries through into the actual model fitting.

```{r}
#| label: baggingR model
baggingR_model <- rand_forest(
  mode = "regression",
  engine = "ranger"
) |>
  set_args(
    seed = 121, # set inside set_args
    mtry = .cols(), # use every predictor
    importance = "permutation"
  )
# you can use .cols() or .preds(); .preds() works before dummy variables are created and .cols() works afterwards

```

1. Explain what `importance = "permutation"` means.

```{r}
#| label: recipe for baggingR
baggingR_recipe <- recipe(
  salary ~ G +  R + H + X2B + X3B + HR + RBI +
    SB + CS + BB + SO + IBB + HBP + SH + SF + GIDP +
    age + lgID, # response ~ predictors
  data = batting_train
)

baggingR_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(baggingR_model) |>
  add_recipe(baggingR_recipe)
```

Since we have not tuned anything, and decision trees rarely require pre-processing, we can go straight to fitting:

```{r}
#| label: fit baggingR model
baggingR_fit <- baggingR_wflow |>
  fit(data = batting_train)
baggingR_fit
```
OOB prediction error
```{r}
#| label: out-of-bag prediction error for baggingR
baggingR_engine <- baggingR_fit |> extract_fit_engine()
baggingR_engine |>
  pluck("prediction.error")
```

2. What is OOB prediction error and how is it computed?
For each obs. in the training set find all trees for which this obs. is not in the bootstrap sample. (should be 30-40% of trees)
Predict y-hat using only these trees.
Calculate MSE using these y-hat values.
Let's look at our calibration plot:

```{r}
#| label: calibration on training data baggingR
baggingR_pred_check <- tibble(
  salary = batting_train$salary,
  .pred = baggingR_engine |> pluck("predictions")
)

ggplot(
  data = baggingR_pred_check,
  mapping = aes(x = salary, y= .pred)
) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0)
```

3. How are these predictions made? Why do we know the predicted values despite not doing any cross-validation?

Bagging can be useful for feature selection; we can use a variable importance plot to identify the most "important" variables.

```{r}
#| label: variable importance plot for baggingR
baggingR_engine |>
  pluck("variable.importance")
vip(baggingR_engine)
```

4. What does a negative value for variable importance in this model mean?
Variable importance :"Permutation" what does this really mean? 
For each predictor:
1. we are going to randomly reshuffle the values
2. Record the increase in OOB error rate when predicting with these permutations as opposed to the original ones. 


Often the actual importance is not that important; what we're looking at is the *relative* importance. We can get this on the variable importance plot automatically by changing the `scale` argument to make the longest bar go out to 100:

```{r}
#| label: relative importance for baggingR
vip(baggingR_engine, scale = TRUE)

```

Finally we make and evaluate our predictions on the test set:

```{r}
#| label: augment baggingR
baggingR_preds <- baggingR_fit |>
  augment(new_data = batting_test)|>
  dplyr :: select(
    nameFirst, nameLast, salary, .pred
  )

head(baggingR_preds)
rmse(baggingR_preds, truth = salary, estimate = .pred)
```

Notice that this is RMSE, and the out-of-bag prediction error was reported as MSE, so we have to transform one of them to compare the two directly. We should find that the out-of-bag prediction error is slightly lower.

### Random Forests for Regression

If we want to use random forests, then we just need to tune the value of `mtry` instead of set it to the number of predictors. We'll use `update` to tell R that we want to slightly alter the bagging model.

```{r}
#| label: update baggingR for RF
rFR_model <- update(baggingR_model, mtry = tune())

rFR_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(rFR_model) |>
  add_recipe(baggingR_recipe)

```

Now we do have to tune the model:

```{r}
#| label: tune rfR model
n_predictors <- sum(baggingR_recipe$var_info$role == "predictor")

rFR_grid <- expand.grid(mtry = seq(1, n_predictors))

rFR_tune <- tune_grid(
  rFR_wflow,
  grid = rFR_grid,
  resamples = batting_kfold,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse)
)
```

```{r}
#| label: investigate cv results rfR
autoplot(rFR_tune)
```


1. Do you expect the models with a smaller or larger value of `mtry` to take longer to fit? Why?

```{r}
#| label: select best rfR model
rFR_final <- select_by_one_std_err(
  rFR_tune,
  metric = "rmse",
  mtry
)

rFR_final
```

Now we finalize our workflow and fit the model on the training set:

```{r}
#| label: fit final rfR model
rFR_wflow_final <- rFR_wflow |>
  finalize_workflow(parameters = rFR_final)
rFR_fit <- fit(rFR_wflow_final, data = batting_train)
```

We still like to get the out-of-bag prediction error and look at the model calibration and variable importance:

```{r}
#| label: investigate OOB predictions
rFR_fit |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("prediction.error")
```

Again, note that the bootstrap is doing the work of making out-of-sample predictions, rather than cross-validation.

```{r}
#| label: relative importance plot rfR
vip(rFR_fit |> extract_fit_engine(), scale = TRUE)
```

2. Compare the variable importance plot for the bagging model to the variable importance plot for the random forest model. Why do you think some variables have quite different importance in the two models?

### Bagging and Random Forests for Classification

```{r}
#| label: rf for classification
rfC_model <- rand_forest(
  mode = "classification", 
  engine = "ranger"
  ) |>
  set_args(seed = 395,
           importance = "permutation",
           mtry = tune()
  )
```


```{r}
#| label: RMP classification recipe
rfC_recipe <- recipe(
hotness ~ scientific_age + gender + interest + 
  uni_control + mentions_accent + discipline + uni_type,  
  data = RMP_train
)


rfC_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(rfC_model) |>
  add_recipe(rfC_recipe)
```

Now we tune the model:

```{r}
#| label: tune rfC model
# I'm sure there's a better way, but this works
n_predictorsC <- sum(rfC_recipe$var_info$role == "predictor")
manual_gridC <- expand.grid(mtry = seq(1, n_predictorsC))

rfC_tune1 <- tune_grid(rfC_model, 
                      rfC_recipe, 
                      resamples = RMP_kfold, 
                      metrics = metric_set(mn_log_loss, accuracy),
                      grid = manual_gridC)

```

```{r}
#| label: investigate tuning results rfC
autoplot(rfC_tune1)
```


Here it looks like considering two predictors maximizes cross-validated accuracy and minimizes mean log-loss.

```{r}
#| label: select best rfC model
rfC_best <- select_best(
  rfC_tune1,
  metric = "mn_log_loss"
)

rfC_best

```

Now we finalize our workflow and fit the model on the training set:

```{r}
#| label: fit best rfC model
rfC_wflow_final <- finalize_workflow(rfC_wflow, parameters = rfC_best) 
rfC_fit <- fit(rfC_wflow_final, data = RMP_train)
rfC_fit
```

1. What classification accuracy metric is being used to evaluate the OOB prediction error for a classification model with `ranger`?

```{r}
#| label: plot OOB brier score and vip for rfC
rfC_engine <- rfC_fit |> extract_fit_engine()

rfC_engine |> pluck("prediction.error")

vip(rfC_engine, scale = TRUE)

```

### Bagging for Missing Data Imputation

Decision trees deal with missing predictor values just fine, but since they are extremely high-variance, we typically don't want to impute missing data using a single tree. Bagging is generally preferred to random forests for missing data imputation.

The function for imputing with bagging in tidymodels is `step_impute_bag()`. You prep and bake the imputation just like we did with `step_impute_linear()` in the Missing Data activity.

The recipe step ` step_impute_bag(all_predictors(), impute_with = imp_vars(all_predictors()))` will impute all of the missing predictor data at once.

## Boosted Trees

Rather than growing trees in parallel, boosting models grow trees in series, with each new tree being fit on the previous tree's errors.

There are many different packages that run their own variants of gradient-boosted trees. As far as I am aware, the most popular version for a while has been the `xgboost` (eXtreme Gradient BOOSTing) algorithm, which is implemented in a number of languages including R and Python. We'll use the `tidymodels` interface to `xgboost`.

### Parameters to Tune in `xgboost`

There are 8 tunable parameters. The ones we aren't going to worry about:

* `mtry` indicates the number of predictors that will be randomly selected at each split - the default is to use all the predictors, but if you have a ton of predictors you may want to tune this.
* `min_n` and `loss_reduction` jointly determine whether a node is a terminal node - if the number of observations at a node is below `min_n`, or all possible splits decrease the loss function by less than `loss_reduction`, the node is a terminal node. The default for `min_n` is 1 and the default for `loss_reduction` is 0. Larger values will give more conservative models.
* `sample_size` is the proportion of observations available to be selected each time a new tree is fit. See the *Stochastic Gradient Descent* section of my course notes.
* `stop_iter` governs what happens if the model gets stuck in a rut - if the model does not improve after `stop_iter` number of iterations, the fitting process stops even if not all the trees have been fit

The ones we care about:

* `trees` indicates the number of trees to fit, or in other words, the number of iterations to run the model for - the default is 15. This should almost always be tuned because when this value is too large we tend to overfit.
* `learn_rate` controls how much each new tree contributes to the estimate of $f$ - the default is 0.3. This should be tuned every time you tune the value of `trees`.
* `tree_depth` controls the interaction depth of the trees - the default is 6, which can support up to six-way interactions. If you tune this, make sure you include a value of 1 (purely additive model) in your grid.

### Regression with `tidymodels` and `xgboost`

`xgboost` cannot work with categorical variables, so we need to convert to indicator variables. However, we do not need to standardize the quantitative predictor variables: 

```{r}
#| label: xgboost regression model
xgboostR_model <- boost_tree(
  mode = "regression",
  engine = "xgboost",
  trees = tune(), #number of trees to fit
  tree_depth = tune(),# interaction depth- how many variables can we split on before getting to a terminal node
  learn_rate = tune()# how fast our trees learn from the previous tress mistakes
)
```
Boosted Trees
1. fit a terrible decision tree
2. compute residuals for this tree
3.add "a bit" of the predictions from this tree to the overall pred. and subtract the equivalent amount from the residuals
4. fit a new tree on the new residuals from step 3.
5.repeat steps 2-4




```{r}
#| label: xgboostR recipe
xgboostR_recipe <- recipe(
  salary ~ G +  R + H + X2B + X3B + HR + RBI + 
    SB + CS + BB + SO + IBB + HBP + SH + SF + 
    GIDP + age + lgID, # response ~ predictors
  data = batting_train
) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

xgboostR_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(xgboostR_model) |>
  add_recipe(xgboostR_recipe)
```

Because we have a bunch of tuning parameters, it's recommended to set up your tuning grid manually. However, if you really have no idea what parts of the parameter space you should be searching in (which is not unusual), you can use an algorithm such as `grid_max_entropy` or `grid_space_filling` that will ensure that the optimal solution is "not too far" from a set of parameter values in the grid; you might then consider setting up a manual grid once you have an idea of where to "zoom in". Because those algorithms do some random sampling from the parameter space to determine the grid, it's best to set a seed before doing the tuning if you're using those functions. 

The tuning does take a bit of time to run:

```{r}
#| label: tune xgboostR
xgboostR_tune <- tune_grid(
  xgboostR_wflow,
  resamples = batting_kfold,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse),
  grid = grid_space_filling(
    trees(),
    tree_depth(),
    learn_rate(),
    size = 20
  )
)
```

trees(): low values lead to high bias, too many trees may lead to overfitting.
tree_depth() : large values can handle very complex interactions which means we are dealing with high variance. The cons are the bigger it is the longer it takes to fit and a less interpretable model. 
often tree depth of 1 works well these trees are called stumps 
learn_rate: small values are going to learn slowly so we will have a higher bias.Larger values mean a higher variance, and it may over fit if the learning rate is to high.
What this means practically is that we want to tune the number of trees and the learning rate together.



Let's get the best model:

```{r}
#| label: investigate best xgboostR model
xgboost_final <- select_by_one_std_err(
  xgboostR_tune,
  metric = "rmse",
  tree_depth,
  trees,
  desc(learn_rate)
)
xgboost_final
```

When we have to choose between models of similar cross-validated RMSE:

* Models with lower interaction depth are simpler
* Models with fewer trees and a higher learning rate tend to fit faster

I tend to prioritize low interaction depth over the other two parameters, but I'm not sure if that's "best practice."

```{r}
#| label: finalize best xgboostR model
xgboost_wflow_final <- xgboostR_wflow |>
  finalize_workflow(parameters = xgboost_final)
```

```{r}
#| label: fit and predict final xgboostR model
xgboost_fit <- fit(xgboost_wflow_final, data = batting_train)
xgboost_predict <- augment(xgboost_fit, new_data = batting_test)
```

Because boosted trees are just another type of tree ensemble, we can still get variable importance out:

```{r}
#| label: vip plot for xgboostR
vip(
  xgboost_fit |> extract_fit_engine(),
  scale = TRUE
)
```

1. Compare the variable importance plot for the boosted model to the variable importance plot for the bagging and random forest models. Why do you think some variables have quite different importance in the different models?

### Classification with `tidymodels` and `xgboost`

`xgboost` can take a long time to tune. Here we're going to get a smaller training set so that it doesn't take forever.

```{r}
#| label: mini training and test sets
set.seed(1000)
# slice_sample takes a random sample of the rows
RMP_train_mini <- dplyr::slice_sample(RMP_train, n = 500)
RMP_test_mini <- dplyr::slice_sample(RMP_test, n = 250)
RMP_kfold_mini <- vfold_cv(
  data = RMP_train_mini,
  v = 5, repeats = 3
)
```

```{r}
#| label: xgboost classification model
xgboostC_model <- boost_tree(
  mode = "classification", 
  engine = "xgboost",
  trees = tune(), 
  tree_depth = tune(),
  learn_rate = tune()
)
```

```{r}
#| label: xgboost recipe with step_bin2factor
# Categorical predictors here, must use step_dummy()
xgboostC_recipe <- recipe(
hotness ~ scientific_age + gender + interest + 
  uni_control + mentions_accent + discipline + uni_type,  
  data = RMP_train
)  |>
  step_bin2factor(all_logical_predictors()) |> # step_dummy will not work with logical variables, we must convert to factor before using step_dummy
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) # non-numeric variables must be converted to numeric to use in xgboost

xgboostC_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(xgboostC_model) |>
  add_recipe(xgboostC_recipe)
```

```{r}
#| label: tune parameters xgboostC
set.seed(148)
xgboostC_tune <- tune_grid(xgboostC_model, 
                      xgboostC_recipe, 
                      resamples = RMP_kfold_mini,
                      metrics = metric_set(mn_log_loss, accuracy),
                      grid = grid_space_filling(
                        trees(), 
                        tree_depth(c(1,3)), # must specify lower and upper bounds, here we don't go beyond 3-way interactions
                        learn_rate(), 
                        size = 18)) # search over 18 possible combinations of the three parameters

```

```{r}
#| label: investigate tuning results xgboostC
autoplot(xgboostC_tune)
```

Notice that we now have a grid showing each metric in its own row and each tuning parameter in its own column. So each model is technically represented 6 times (one per figure), which can make finding the "best" model a bit complicated.

```{r}
#| label: select best xgboostC model
xgboostC_best <- select_by_one_std_err(
  xgboostC_tune,
  metric = "mn_log_loss",
 tree_depth, trees, desc(learn_rate)
)

xgboostC_best

```

Now we finalize our workflow and fit the model on the training set:

```{r}
#| label: finalize and fit xgboostC
xgboostC_wflow_final <- finalize_workflow(xgboostC_wflow, parameters = xgboostC_best) 

xgboostC_fit <- fit(xgboostC_wflow_final, data = RMP_train)
xgboostC_fit

```

2. What is this data frame showing?

```{r}
#| label: predict and vip with xgboostC
xgboostC_predict <- augment(xgboostC_fit, new_data = RMP_test_mini)
xgboostC_predict |>
  dplyr::select(.pred_class, .pred_hot, .pred_cold, hotness, scientific_age, gender, everything())

mn_log_loss(xgboostC_predict, truth = hotness, .pred_hot, event_level = "first")

xgboostC_fit |> extract_fit_engine() |>
      vip(scale = TRUE)
```
